subparts.text: Why is consistency important in financial reporting? Provide examples of how inconsistent application of accounting policies could affect stakeholders. 1

State with reasons whether the following are Capital Expenditure or Revenue Expenditure: Expenses incurred in connection with obtaining a license for starting the factory were ₹10,000. ₹1,000 paid for removal of stock to a new site. Rings and Pistons of an engine were changed at a cost of ₹ 5,000 to get full efficiency 2
Explain the principle of 'Lower of Cost or Net Realizable Value ' as per AS-2 3
Calculate the gross profit ratio from the following data: Sales= ₹85000 Opening Stock= ₹9050 Closing Stock= ₹14000 Purchases= ₹ 54525 Carriage Inwards= ₹1425 4
Identify the different users of financial reports. How does each user group utilize the information provided? 1
What is working capital, and why is its management important for a business? 4
Which errors are not detected by the trial balance. Explain with examples 2
Explain the methods of amortization for intangible assets as per AS- 26. 3

questions.question_text: What are International Financial Reporting Standards , and why are they important for global financial reporting? 1
Explain the significance of accounting principles in financial reporting. Discuss any four fundamental accounting principles with examples. 1

From the following particulars prepare a Balance Sheet of Mr. X, for the year ended 31st March, 2013. Capital: ₹ 2,00,000: Drawings: ₹40,000; Cash in Hand: ₹20,000; Loan from Bank: ₹ 40,000; Sundry Creditors: ₹ 40,000; Bills Payable: ₹20,000; Bank Overdraft: ₹20,000; Goodwill: ₹60,000; Sundry Debtors: ₹80,000; Land and Building: ₹50,000; Plant and Machinery: ₹ 80,000; Investment: ₹20,000; Bills Receivable: ₹10,000. The following adjustments are made at the time of preparing final accounts: I. Outstanding Liabilities for: Salaries ₹10,000; wages ₹20,000; Interest on Bank Overdraft ₹3,000; and Interest on Bank Loan ₹6,000. II. Provide Interest on Capital @ 10% p.a. III. Depreciation on Plant and Machinery by 10% p.a. IV. Bad Debts amounted to ₹10,000 and make a provision for Bad Debts @ 10% on Sundry Debtors. V. Closing stock amounted to ₹1,20,000. Net profit for the year amounted to ₹96,000 after considering all the above adjustments. 2

From the following ledger account balances, prepare a Trial Balance of Mr. Sen for the year ended 31st March,2013. Capital ₹80,000 ; Sales ₹10,00,000; Adjusted Purchase ₹8,00,000; Current A/c ₹10,000; Petty Cash ₹10,000; Sales Ledger Balance ₹1,20,000; Purchase Ledger Balance ₹60,000; Salaries ₹24,000; Carriage Inwards ₹4,000; Carriage Outward ₹6,000; Discount Allowed ₹10,000; Building ₹80,000; Outstanding Expenses ₹10,000; Prepaid Insurance ₹2,000 ; Depreciation ₹4,000 ; Cash at Bank ₹80,000 ; Loan A/c ₹66,000; Profit & Loss A/c ₹20,000; Bad Debts Recovered ₹2,000 ; Stock at 31.03.2013 - ₹1,20,000; Interest Received ₹10,000; Accrued Interest ₹4,000; Investment ₹20,000; Provision for Bad Debts ₹6,000 ; General Reserve ₹20,000. 2

Prepare a store ledger account on the basis of following information by following the weighted average method Rate per unit January 1, 2017 January 10, 2017 January 15, 2017 January 20, 2017 January 25, 2017 Received 500 units Received 300 units Issued 700 units Received 400 units Issued 300 units 20 24 -- 28 -- 3 January 27, 2017 January 31, 2017 Received 500 units Issued 200 units 22 --
Explain AS-9 Revenue Recognition. What is the scope of AS-9, and which types of revenue does it cover? 3

From the following information, calculate the Net Cash Flow from Investing Activities: Particulars Opening ₹ Closing ₹ Machinery Accumulated Depreciation Patents 4,00,000 1,00,000 2,80,000 4,20,000 1,10,000 1,60,000 Additional information: During the year, a machine costing ₹40,000 with an accumulated depreciation of ₹ 24,000 was sold for ₹20,000. Patents were written off to the extent of ₹40,000 and some patents were sold at a profit of ₹20,000 4
